paragraphs.body: It was a hot humid summer in Korea. I was walking around a small alley way that had a lot of small, cute stores with my mom. And my mom and I walked into this one store that had a lot of cute and fancy figurines that we like to collect and interesting looking magnets to stick on our fridge. We walked around the store for a little bit and came across a whole keychain section. The whole wall was filled with so many keychains, and they weren’t like the same keychains there were a variety of them. I’ve never seen this many keychains on a wall. All the keychains were different from each other that every time I looked over at a different section of the wall it was just a whole new world of keychains. Some of them would be goofy with silly faces or some cute characters that was popular at that time. But out of all these keychains my mom specifically chose one that represents Korea, because she wants me to be proud of who I am. This keychain was a cultural little pink and yellow sac with a gold pig and a Korean flag on it. Many people used to wear them back in the day as a good luck charm but without the flag. My mom got this keychain for me so that I have some luck while I’m in America away from her. She wanted to support me in some way that she got me this little keychain that I could carry around easily anywhere I wanted to, to make it seem like she’s always there next to me.

annotations.text: It was a hot humid summer in Korea. I was walking around a small alley way that had a lot of small, cute stores with my mom.
annotations.comment: Yes! Nice job starting with narrative.
annotations.context_right: And my mom and I wa

paragraphs.body: Ever since I was in 6th grade, I grew up living with my siblings only. I originally lived with my mom and dad in Korea with my brother, but my mom said I whined to go back to America, so I ended up going back with my dad and my brother. My mom had to remain in Korea because of her work. But two years later we had some other family issues going on that my dad had to go back to Korea to take care of it causing me to live with my siblings in Boston.

annotations.text: 6th grade, I grew up living with my siblings only. I
annotations.comment: Nice transition and great job with context!

paragraphs.body: This keychain that my mom gifted me is a very special gift that I can never forget about it. It would be light as a feather except for the Korean flag. The Korean flag was the only part that gave the extra weight to the key chain. Every time you hold the sac you can feel the soft luxury cotton rubbing against your hand feeling like a mini pillow, but at the same time you would feel the coldness rubbing against you when the golden pig swung by. Even though it felt luxurious when it touched me, I would get a whiff of the cheap factory smell passing right by me, but the Korean flag didn’t. The small gold pig painted in fake gold paint with a cover of high-gloss paint to make it look exquisite, radiating that strong smell of paint and having that bit of plastic texture, but the Korean flag didn’t. The sac filled with cotton, having strings of thread popping out between the pink and yellow patterns with all the cotton poking out looking like it could fall apart any moment, but the Korean flag didn’t, it was staying strong and sturdy with no sight of paint chipped off, none of that strong chemical paint smell radiating off of it, only the smell of metal. I can vividly see the red and blue colors on the core of the flag with the black stripes in the corners perfectly projecting the Korean flag, outlined with gold all around it. No matter what it bumps into it would stay strong with no sight of paint chipping off or indents.

annotations.text: The Korean flag was the only part that gave the extra weight to the key chain.
annotations.comment: interesting because it feels like emotional weight as well. Nice metaphor
I love these repetitions! You're really doing an excellent job promoting the importance, and the metaphor, of the flag.

paragraphs.body: This goes back to when I was entering middle school, and my parents had to permanently stay in Korea. I won’t have the warm Korean food my mom always cooked for me. No more gyelanbab she made for me every morning. No more miyeokguk she made for me every birthday. No more kimchibokkembab she made as a snack every other day. None of them. I won’t have my dad who would always cook late night snacks for us before we watched Korean shows either. No more running man with tteokbokki. No more gag concert with ojinguh. No more quality time with delicious food. I have to eat all the American greasy food served now. I have to watch Korean shows all by myself. If I want to eat warm Korean food, I have to learn how to cook it or even just look for restaurants that will serve them. And now I was about to lose the two people who always reminded me of who I am deep inside. I was about to have a whole independent life by myself in America with my older siblings only. And because I won’t have my parents who will remind me of my culture, she wanted to give me a small little gift so that I don’t forget who I am. Even if the material felt cheap all that mattered to me was that the Korean flag was the sturdiest part of the keychain because I have a fear of losing my Korean identity while living in America. I have something I can represent to the world of who I am while being fearless.

annotations.text: No more gyelanbab she made for me every morning. No more miyeokguk she made for me every birthday. No more kimchibokkembab she made as a snack every other day. None of them. I won’t have my dad who would always cook late night snacks for us before we watched Korean shows either. No more running man with tteokbokki. No more gag concert with ojinguh.

paragraphs.body: When I went to school, everybody always asked if I was Japanese or Chinese. They never asked if I was Korean. I started to hide the fact that I’m Korean because not many people knew about Korean people at that time. But that’s when I was able to confidently show my keychain with the Korean flag on it. I knew if my mom was next to me, she would be proud of me this moment because I’m not forgetting about who I am and where I come from. If I didn’t have this keychain by my side, I might’ve felt embarrassed and not be able to speak up on who I am. And by this keychain always sticking next to me, it gave me confidence on who I should be and constantly reminding me of my culture, allowing me to make some Korean friends who would also always remind me where I come from. Whenever I go over to my friends home their parents would happily greet me and share the food, they cooked up for me. Thinking back at this moment I’m thankful that my mom got me this keychain because if I didn’t, maybe I would’ve forgotten my own culture and not know anything about it. But even till this day I’m happy that my culture is stuck with me, and I can be proud of who I am. I think if my mom never gifted me this keychain, I would’ve felt very disappointed in myself for not staying connected to my culture and possibly lose a part of myself. And because Korea is more well known today, I’m proud to say that I’m Korean and that I know their culture and know how to speak Korean.

Being Korean does not have anything to do with behavior. Being Korean does not have anything to do with personality. Being Korean does not have anything to do with just listening to their music. Just because you act Koren doesn’t mean you are Korean right? It’s all about the culture and language Korea has. Korea has a huge culture on etiquette and how to be respectful towards elders. How you should be obedient. It’s also all about how deeply you are connected to the culture and knowing the history of what they have gone through as a country. Korea values hard working and diligent people. You have to know all the hardships they have gone through in the past and present to see how long it took them to build this country as it is now. This is why Korea is known for having hardworking and diligent people. You have to know how Korea can unify people to fight for their rights and freedom. How they can fight for their peace in the world. Being Korean is how their culture can influence their values and practices.

annotations.comment: Nice job defining all the characteristics that make you feel proud and fearless as a Korean woman.
annotations.context_left: towards elders. How you should be obedient. It’s also all about how deeply you are connected to

paragraphs.body: It's important to keep close contact with the culture you grew up in because it is a thankful thing to have in your life and getting to know better about where you come from and not be embarrassed about who you are. Instead of being a part of the threatened group of cultural diversity it’s better to stay connected and be proud of who you are. So many people who come internationally forget where they truly come from and always regret that they forget about their own culture. By you staying connected to your culture it can allow you to spread your culture even more and maybe even teach those who lost their culture.
